likely_position: Counsel would most likely take the conservative, risk-minimizing position: advise Okafor to recuse from both the RFP-framework and award votes as a baseline, and separately urge resignation from the Harbor Light board to eliminate that independent source of conflict — but caution that recusal likely does NOT cure the § 1090 problem, because the spouse's salary (a community-property interest attributed to Okafor under Gov. Code § 82030 and § 1090 case law) makes Okafor "financially interested" in any Harbor Light contract, no § 1091/1091.5 exception cleanly fits, the "rule of necessity" is unavailable since two other bidders exist, and courts (e.g., Thomson v. Call; People v. Honig; Stigall) treat § 1090 violations as potentially voiding the whole contract regardless of the interested member's abstention. Given § 1090's harsh remedies (void ab initio, disgorgement, misdemeanor exposure under § 1097, forfeiture) versus the PRA's comparatively narrower § 87100/87103 disqualification-plus-recusal regime, counsel will likely flag § 1090 as the dominant risk and recommend the city not proceed with awarding to Harbor Light this cycle absent restructuring (e.g., removing council discretion from the award, walling spousal compensation from city-funded programs) or a favorable FPPC/AG advice letter — i.e., counsel gives a hedged "recuse and resign, but that may not be enough" answer rather than a clean go-ahead.
